sentences: The Maine Red Claws are a professional minor league basketball team based in Portland, Maine.
The Red Claws compete in the NBA G League and are affiliated with the Boston Celtics.
The team played its first season in 2009–10.
The team's home court is the Portland Expo Building.
The team is owned by Maine Basketball, LLC, which is chaired by William Ryan, Jr.
The Red Claws made their first playoff appearance on April 6, 2013, as the eighth seeded team and was swept by the Rio Grande Valley Vipers in the opening round.
The NBA G League All-Star Game is an annual exhibition basketball game held by the NBA G League.
The G League was founded in 2001 as the National Basketball Development League (NBDL) and later as the NBA Development League (D-League).
The league adopted its current name at the start of the 2017–18 season.
The league serves as the National Basketball Association's official minor league basketball organization.
Raptors 905 are a Canadian professional basketball team in the NBA G League.
The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario, and began play in the 2015–16 season.

The club, which is the G League affiliate of the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association, plays their home games at the Hershey Centre, replacing the Centre's former basketball tenant, the Mississauga Power of the National Basketball League of Canada.
The team also plays occasional home games at the Air Canada Centre, the home of their parent club.
Raptors 905 were the eighth D-League team to be owned by an NBA team and the first to be based outside the United States.
The Washington NBA G League team is an American professional basketball team in the NBA G League and an affiliate of the Washington Wizards of the National Basketball Association.
Based in Washington, D.C., the team will play their home games during the 2018–19 season at the St. Elizabeths East Entertainment and Sports Arena.
The team became the twenty-third G League team to be owned by an NBA team.
The NBA G League is the National Basketball Association's official minor league basketball organization.
The league was known as the National Basketball Development League (NBDL) from 2001 to 2005, and the NBA Development League (NBA D-League) from 2005 until 2017.

The league started with eight teams until NBA commissioner David Stern announced a plan to expand the NBA D-League to fifteen teams and develop it into a true minor league farm system, with each NBA D-League team affiliated with one or more NBA teams in March 2005.
At the conclusion of the 2013–14 NBA season, 33% of NBA players had spent time in the NBA D-League, up from 23% in 2011.
As of the 2017–18 season, the league consists of 26 teams, all of which are either single-affiliated or owned by an NBA team.
These are regular season standings and playoff results for the NBA G League.
The NBA G League is the official minor league basketball organization owned and run by the National Basketball Association (NBA).
The league was formed in 2001 as the National Basketball Development League (NBDL).
The league was renamed to NBA Development League (NBA D-League) in 2005 to reflect its close affiliation with the NBA.
In 2017, it was renamed NBA G League, as part of a sponsorship deal with Gatorade.
Edward J. "E. J." Singler (born June 6, 1990) is an American professional basketball player for Raptors 905 of the NBA G League.
He played college basketball for the University of Oregon.
As a senior at Oregon, he was named to the All-Pac-12 first team.
Kyle Edward Singler (born May 4, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Oklahoma City Thunder of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
Singler was a four-year starter for the Duke men's basketball team and was instrumental in their 2010 NCAA championship run, earning MVP of the Final Four.
In the 2011 NBA draft, the Detroit Pistons selected Singler in the second round with the overall 33rd pick.
Singler joined the Pistons for the 2012–13 season after playing in Spain during 2011–12.
The Iowa Wolves are an American professional basketball team based in Des Moines, Iowa.
It is owned by and affiliated with the Minnesota Timberwolves as of the 2017–18 season.
They play in the Western Conference in the NBA G League, a minor league basketball organization run by the National Basketball Association (NBA).
The Wolves play their home games at the Wells Fargo Arena.
From 2007 to 2017, the team was known as the Iowa Energy in the NBA Development League (D-League) until being purchased and renamed by the Timberwolves.
They broke the D-league attendance record on their first ever home game with 8,842 fans.
They later set the record again in game two of the 2011 D-League Finals with an attendance of 14,036 fans.
They won the 2011 D-League Finals, defeating the Rio Grande Valley Vipers two-games-to-one.
The Fort Wayne Mad Ants are a team of the NBA G League, located in Fort Wayne, Indiana.
The Allen County War Memorial Coliseum is the home venue for the team.
The team is the first minor league basketball franchise to play in Fort Wayne since the Fort Wayne Fury of the Continental Basketball Association.
The Mad Ants won their first D-League championship in 2014.
In September 2015, the Indiana Pacers purchased the Mad Ants, thus becoming the Pacers' one-to-one D-League affiliate.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

hop1_docs: Kyle Singler | Kyle Edward Singler (born May 4, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Oklahoma City Thunder of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  Singler was a four-year starter for the Duke men's basketball team and was instrumental in their 2010 NCAA championship run, earning MVP of the Final Four.  In the 2011 NBA draft, the Detroit Pistons selected Singler in the second round with the overall 33rd pick.  Singler joined the Pistons for the 2012–13 season after playing in Spain during 2011–12.

E. J. Singler | Edward J. "E. J." Singler (born June 6, 1990) is an American professional basketball player for Raptors 905 of the NBA G League.  He played college basketball for the University of Oregon.  As a senior at Oregon, he was named to the All-Pac-12 first team.

Sherri Singler | Sherri Nadine Singler (born February 19, 1974, in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan as Sherri Leonard) is a Canadian curler from Harris, Saskatchewan.  She currently plays second for the Stefanie Lawton team.

Saigon Heat | The Saigon Heat (Vietnamese: "Sài Gòn Heat" ) are a Vietnamese professional basketball team based in Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam.  They play in the ASEAN Basketball League.  Since the 2014 season, the Heat's home games are played at the CIS Arena on the campus of the Canadian International School in District 7.  Saigon Heat is the first professional basketball team based in Vietnam.

Raptors 905 | Raptors 905 are a Canadian professional basketball team in the NBA G League.  The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario, and began play in the 2015–16 season.  The club, which is the G League affiliate of the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association, plays their home games at the Hershey Centre, replacing the Centre's former basketball tenant, the Mississauga Power of the National Basketball League of Canada.  The team also plays occasional home games at the Air Canada Centre, the home of their parent club.  Raptors 905 were the eighth D-League team to be owned by an NBA team and the first to be based outside the United States.

Mississauga Power | The Mississauga Power were a Canadian professional basketball team based in Mississauga, Ontario, that competed in the National Basketball League of Canada (NBL).  Established in as the Oshawa Power, they played in the Central Division.  The Oshawa team began play in the inaugural NBL Canada season, along with the London Lightning, Moncton Miracles, and Summerside Storm and three Premier Basketball League (PBL) teams.

Saint John Riptide | The Saint John Riptide are a Canadian professional basketball team based in Saint John, New Brunswick.  They compete in the National Basketball League of Canada (NBL Canada) as a member of the Atlantic Division.  The Riptide play their home games at Harbour Station.  The Riptide are one of two professional basketball teams from the province of New Brunswick, the other being the Moncton Miracles.  The team was established in 2007 as the Manchester Millrats, an expansion team based in Manchester, New Hampshire, which played in the American Basketball Association (ABA).  They would later move to the Premier Basketball League (PBL) in 2008, relocated to Saint John in 2010, and renamed in 2016.

- Fact: "Raptors 905 are a Canadian professional basketball team ... based in Mississauga, Ontario." [5] (quoted fragment)
- Bridge entity: Raptors 905 — present and unambiguous in passages [2] and [5].
- Missing/ambiguous facts: none.
- Final answer: Mississauga (city). Source: [5].
hop2_docs: Raptors 905 | Raptors 905 are a Canadian professional basketball team in the NBA G League.  The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario, and began play in the 2015–16 season.  The club, which is the G League affiliate of the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association, plays their home games at the Hershey Centre, replacing the Centre's former basketball tenant, the Mississauga Power of the National Basketball League of Canada.  The team also plays occasional home games at the Air Canada Centre, the home of their parent club.  Raptors 905 were the eighth D-League team to be owned by an NBA team and the first to be based outside the United States.

Malcolm Miller (basketball) | Malcolm Miller (born March 6, 1993) is an American professional basketball player for the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  He was signed on a two-way contract with the Raptors' NBA G League affiliate Raptors 905.  He played college basketball for Holy Cross.

Lorenzo Brown | Lorenzo Brown (born August 26, 1990) is an American professional basketball player for the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association (NBA), on a two-way contract with the Raptors' NBA G League affiliate, the Raptors 905.  He played college basketball for North Carolina State University.

Jerry Stackhouse | Jerry Darnell Stackhouse (born November 5, 1974) is an American retired professional basketball player who played 18 seasons in the National Basketball Association (NBA) and is currently the head coach for the Raptors 905, the Toronto Raptors NBA G League team.  He has also worked as an NBA TV analyst.

2014–15 Mississauga Power season | The 2014–15 Mississauga Power season was the fourth and final season of the franchise in the National Basketball League of Canada (NBL).  It was also their second season in Mississauga.  Following an unsuccessful season with a record of 7–25, the Power folded, resulting in the creation of Raptors 905, a new NBA Development League affiliate to the NBA's Toronto Raptors.
Nicki Gross | Nicki Gross (born July 24, 1989) is an assistant coach for Raptors 905 of the NBA Development League.  She is currently the only female coach in the D-League.

Christian Watford | Christian Ramon Watford (born April 28, 1991) is an American professional basketball player for the Raptors 905 of the NBA G League.  He played college basketball for Indiana University.
response.summary_2: E. J. Singler is an American professional basketball player for Raptors 905 of the NBA G League. Raptors 905 are a Canadian NBA G League team based in Mississauga, Ontario.
